# Chapter 1. The Process of Production

```{=html}
<p class="fst">In the first section we moved for the most part on the commodity market; we saw how commodities are exchanged, sold and bought; how money performs the most varied functions; how money becomes capital as soon as it finds on the market the commodity labour-power.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The capitalist has bought labour-power, and with the new acquisition withdraws from the <em>market</em>, where for the time being it is of no use to him at all, to the place where he can consume it, put it to use, into the <em>workplace.</em> Let us follow him there. Let us leave the sphere of <em>commodity circulation</em> and look about us in the sphere of <em>production.</em> It is within this sphere that the following discussion shall move.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>“The use of labour-power is labour itself.” The capitalist consumes the labour-power he has bought by having its seller work for him, produce commodities.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Commodity-producing labour has, as we already saw in the first section, two sides: it is the creator of use-values and of commodity-values. As a creator of use-values, labour is not a particular peculiarity of commodity production, but a constant necessity for the human race, independent of every particular social form. As such, labour displays three moments: <em>1. a purposeful and purpose-serving activity of the human being; 2. the object of labour; 3. the means of labour.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Labour is a purpose-serving and purposeful activity of the human being, an action of the human being upon the material of nature in order to give it a form serviceable for his needs. The elements of such an activity are already to be found in the animal kingdom, but only at a certain stage of development of the human race does it wholly strip off its instinctive form and become a purposeful activity. All labour is not merely muscular labour, but also labour of the brain and nerves. Marx aptly remarks: “Besides the exertion of the organs that work, the purposeful will, which manifests itself as attention, is required for the whole duration of the labour, and all the more, the less the labour, by its own content and the manner of its execution, carries the worker along with it, the less, therefore, he enjoys it as the play of his own bodily and mental powers.”</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The worker acts upon an object, the <em>object of labour;</em> in this activity he employs auxiliary means, things whose mechanical, physical or chemical properties he causes to act upon the object of labour in accordance with his purposes; these auxiliary means are the <em>means of labour.</em> The result of working up the object of labour with the aid of the means of labour is the <em>product.</em> Means of labour and object of labour are <em>means of production.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>When a joiner makes a table, he works up wood in doing so. If the object of labour is not found in nature, as, for example, the tree in the primeval forest, but if labour has already been necessary to obtain it—for example, in the case before us the labour of felling and transporting the wood—then it is called <em>raw material.</em> The wood in our example is <em>raw material,</em> as are the glue, the paint, the varnish that are worked up in the making of the table. The wood is the <em>principal material;</em> glue, paint, varnish are auxiliary substances. Plane, saw, and so forth, on the other hand, are <em>means of labour,</em> the table is the <em>product.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">“Whether a use-value appears as raw material, means of labour or product depends entirely on its particular function in the labour-process, on the place it occupies within it, and with the change of this place those determinations change.”</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">A head of cattle, for example, can function successively as <em>product</em> (of cattle-breeding), <em>means of labour</em> (for example, as a draught animal) and <em>raw material</em> (in fattening).</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The means of labour are of the highest importance for the development of the human race. The manner of producing depends first and foremost on them; but every mode of production conditions social relations peculiar to it, with a corresponding juridical, religious, philosophical and artistic superstructure.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under every mode of production, means of production (object of labour and means of labour) and labour-power constitute the necessary elements of the production of <em>use-values,</em> that is, of the <em>labour-process.</em> But the <em>social character</em> of this process (procedure) is a different one under the various modes of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us now examine how it takes shape under the capitalist mode of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>For the commodity producer the production of <em>use-values</em> is only a means to the end of producing <em>commodity-values.</em> The commodity is a unity of use-value and value; he cannot therefore produce values unless he produces use-values. The commodities he produces must satisfy a need, must have some use for someone, otherwise he cannot dispose of them. The circumstance that his commodity must be a use-value is, however, for the commodity producer only a necessary evil, not the ultimate purpose of his business activity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The production process of commodity production is therefore at one and the same time the process of producing <em>use-values</em> and <em>commodity-values;</em> it is a unity of <em>labour-process</em> and <em>value-forming process.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This holds for commodity production in general. Now, however, we have to observe the production process in a particular kind of commodity production: the production of commodities by means of <em>purchased labour-power</em> for the purpose of obtaining a <em>surplus value.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>How, then, does the <em>labour process</em> take shape?</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>At first it is, in essence, not altered by the intervention of the capitalist.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us imagine, for example, a weaver who works for himself. His loom belongs to him; he buys the yarn himself; he can work when and how he pleases; the product of his labour is his property. But he grows poor and must sell his loom. On what shall he now live? Nothing remains for him but to hire himself out to a capitalist and to weave for the latter. This capitalist buys his labour-power, buys also the loom and the necessary yarn, and now sets the weaver at his—the capitalist's—loom, so that he may work up the purchased yarn. Perhaps the loom the capitalist bought is the very one that the weaver in his distress had to dispose of. And even if this is not the case, the weaver nevertheless weaves in the same way as before; outwardly the labour process has not changed.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Yet two great changes have occurred: the weaver no longer works for himself, but for the capitalist; the latter now controls the worker at his work, sees to it that he does not work too sluggishly or too carelessly, and so forth. And—the product of the worker's labour now belongs not to him, but to the capitalist.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>These are the immediate effects on the <em>labour process</em> as soon as capital takes possession of the production process. But how does the <em>value-forming process</em> now take shape?</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us first calculate how great the value amounts to of the product which has been produced as a commodity for the capitalist by purchased labour-power with purchased means of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let the capitalist buy the labour-power, we shall assume, for one day. The means of subsistence necessary for the maintenance of the worker are produced in 6 hours of socially necessary labour-time. Let just as much and just such labour-time be embodied in 8 marks. Let the capitalist buy the labour-power at its value; let him pay the worker 3 marks for the working day. <a href="#ab2-kap01-n1" id="ab2-kap01-f1" name="ab2-kap01-f1">[1]</a></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us assume that the capitalist holds cotton yarn to be a <em>use-value</em> that is much sought after and can easily be sold; he therefore decides to have yarn produced, and buys means of labour—for the sake of simplicity we shall here regard these as represented by individual spindles—and cotton. In one pound of cotton let there be, say, two hours of labour contained; it therefore costs 1 mark. From one pound of cotton one pound of yarn is spun. In spinning every 100 pounds of cotton, one spindle is used up, worn out; in spinning 1 pound, therefore, <sup><small>1</small></sup>/<small><small>100</small></small> of a spindle. In one spindle there are 20 hours of labour = 10 marks. In one hour of labour 2 pounds of cotton are spun, in 6 hours therefore 12 pounds—always assuming normal, average, socially necessary conditions of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>How much value will, under these circumstances, be contained in one pound of yarn?</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>First, the value of the cotton and spindles consumed in its production. This passes into the product without diminution or enlargement. The <em>use-value</em> of the cotton and the spindle has become a different one, their <em>value</em> has remained untouched. This becomes clear if one regards the various labour processes required for the production of the final product as successive parts of one and the same labour process. Let us assume that the spinner is also a cotton planter and that the cotton is spun immediately after it is obtained; the yarn now appears as the product of the planter's and the spinner's labour, and its value is measured by the labour-time socially necessary for the production of the cotton and its working up into yarn. Nothing is changed in the value of the product if, other conditions being equal, the labour processes necessary for its production are carried on for the account of different people. The value of the worked-up cotton thus reappears in the yarn; the same holds for the value of the spindles used up. We here leave auxiliary materials aside for the sake of simplicity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To this <em>transferred</em> value there is added the value which the spinner's labour <em>adds</em> to the cotton. In one hour of labour 2 pounds are spun—let us assume that in one mark there are 2 hours of labour. One hour of labour thus forms a value of ½ mark.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The value of 1 pound of yarn is therefore equal to the value of 1 pound of cotton (= 1 mark) + <sup><small>1</small></sup>/<small><small>100</small></small> of a spindle (= <sup><small>1</small></sup>/<small><small>10</small></small> mark) + ½ hour of labour (= ¼ mark), or expressed in marks: 1 + <sup><small>1</small></sup>/<small><small>10</small></small> + ¼ = 1 mark 35 pfennigs.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In 6 hours, accordingly, 12 pounds of yarn are spun, of a value of 16 marks 20 pfennigs. But how much did it cost the capitalist to attain this result? He had to give up 12 pounds of cotton = 12 marks, <sup><small>12</small></sup>/<small><small>100</small></small> of a spindle = 1 mark 20 pfennigs, and 1 labour-power = 3 marks, together 16 marks 20 pfennigs, just as much as he possesses in yarn-value.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p><em>He has thus so far had the work done for nothing;</em> the purchased commodity labour-power has so far procured him no surplus value.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But our capitalist is not to be disconcerted. He has bought the use-value of the labour-power for the <em>whole day</em>; he has bought it honestly and fairly, at its full value; and for that reason the right also belongs to him to make full and complete use of its use-value. It does not occur to him to say to the worker: “I have bought your labour-power with a sum of money in which 6 hours of labour are contained. You have worked 6 hours for me; we are quits, you can go.” Rather he says: “I have bought your labour-power for the whole day, for the whole day it belongs to me; so keep briskly working, as long as you can, without wasting a single moment of the time that is not yours but mine.” And he lets the man work, instead of 6, perhaps 12 hours.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>After a further 6 hours, at the end of the working day, he reckons again. He now possesses 24 pounds of yarn worth 32 marks 40 pfennigs. Among his outlays he counts 24 pounds of cotton = 24 marks, <sup><small>24</small></sup>/<small><small>100</small></small> spindles = 2 marks 40 pfennigs, and 1 labour-power = 3 marks, together 29 marks 40 pfennigs. Smirking, he lays his account book aside. He has gained 8 marks, or, as he expresses it, “earned” them. He has earned them, acquired surplus value, without violating the laws of commodity exchange. The cotton, the spindles, the labour-power, all of them were bought at their value. If he obtains surplus value, it is only because he <em>consumed</em> these bought commodities, not, of course, as <em>means of enjoyment,</em> but as <em>means of production,</em> and because he consumed the use-value of the labour-power he had bought <em>beyond a certain point.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under the system of commodity production the production process is always a <em>value-forming process;</em> no matter whether it is carried on with <em>bought</em> or with <em>one’s own</em> labour-power; but only if it lasts beyond a certain point in time is the value-forming process also a former of <em>surplus value,</em> and as such a <em>valorisation process.</em> The production process must <em>last longer</em> than until the value of the bought labour-power has been replaced by newly created value, if surplus value is to be produced.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The peasant who tills his own field, and the artisan who works on his own account, can also work beyond the time which he is compelled to work in order to replace the means of subsistence he has consumed. He too can therefore produce surplus value, his labour can become a valorisation process. But as soon as the valorisation process is carried on with <em>bought, alien</em> labour-power, it is a <em>capitalist production process;</em> this is from the outset, by its very nature, of necessity and by design, a valorisation process.</p>
```

## Notes

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ab2-kap01-f1" id="ab2-kap01-n1" name="ab2-kap01-n1">1.</a> These and the following figures are of course quite arbitrary, chosen for the sake of easier illustration. This would seem to be self-evident; but many among the many who have written about <strong>Capital</strong> have assumed that Marx adduced <em>examples</em> of the kind given as <em>facts.</em> What the commentators on <strong>Capital</strong> are capable of achieving is shown by the following: In the 57th volume of Herr von Treitschke’s <strong>Preussische Jahrbücher</strong> a certain Dr. R. Stegemann published an article overflowing with shallowness on the <em>Economic Fundamental Conception of Karl Marx.</em> Immediately after he had presented the “value principle” as Marx’s basic <em>demand,</em> he tells us (p. 227): “Marx <em>asserts</em> that human society would need only about six hours of daily labour to procure the means of subsistence indispensable to all, namely if <em>everyone</em> were to work, and indeed <em>according to his abilities.</em>” Of all this there is not a word in <strong>Capital.</strong> Had Herr Stegemann expended less imagination and more attention, he would have found on page 209 (2nd ed.) of <strong>Capital</strong> that Marx calculated the necessary labour which a spinner in a particular spinning mill in the sixties <em>actually</em> had to perform, on the basis of data supplied to him by a manufacturer from Manchester. He arrived at the result that with a ten-hour working time the necessary labour time of the spinner amounted to <em>not quite four hours,</em> the surplus labour time, during which he produced surplus value, to something over six hours. – We shall see later that the labour time necessary for the maintenance of the worker is a very variable magnitude.</p>
```


---

[↑ Previous: Chapter 3. The Transformation of Money into Capital](ab1-kap3.qmd) [↑ Return to Table of Contents](../index.qmd) [Next: Chapter 2. The Role of Capital in the Formation of Value ↓](ab2-kap02.qmd)
